summary: Stancil argues that building software (and living) is dominated by countless small, tedious decisions, and that computers force us to be overly precise about them. He suggests AI’s real appeal isn’t just automating work but relieving decision fatigue by making acceptable choices on our behalf—and persuading us to accept them.
money_quotes: The absence of a choice is itself a choice—a statement, even.
So much of it is the distracting labor of little decisions.
But its emotionally intoxicating power—its real delight, or its real danger—is that it decides stuff for us.
We’re just looking for permission to make a choice, and move on.
key_insight: AI’s most compelling value is not perfecting outcomes but relieving decision fatigue by making and justifying choices for us.
